question_title: How much will Nasdaq-100 Futures total price returns exceed S&P 500 Futures in these biweekly periods of Q1 2026? (Mar 16 - Mar 27)
summary: The NQ-ES 10-trading-day return spread for Mar 16-27, 2026 is forecasted using a blended Monte Carlo simulation combining recent regime data with full history. Key inputs: (1) Full 2020-2026 history shows NQ outperforms ES by +0.21pp on average over 10 days (std ~1.76pp), but (2) the recent 3-month regime shows NQ underperforming at -0.34pp per 10-day window due to tech rotation and tariff concerns. (3) The FOMC meeting on March 17-18 is a major catalyst at the period start — markets are divided on a rate cut vs hold, adding event volatility but with ambiguous directional impact on the NQ-ES spread. (4) NQ beta to ES is ~1.3 recently, amplifying broad market moves in both directions. The blended simulation (40% 3-month, 35% 6-month, 25% full-history parameters) centers near -0.1pp with a ~1.5pp standard deviation, reflecting mild NQ underperformance bias in the current regime while acknowledging substantial two-sided uncertainty from the FOMC event and macro conditions.

factors.description: Full-history (2020-2026) 10-day NQ-ES spread averages +0.21pp (std 1.76pp), reflecting long-run NQ outperformance via tech premium

Recent 3-month regime shows NQ underperformance: daily spread mean -0.034%, implying ~-0.34pp over 10 days. NQ beta ~1.3 with negative alpha reflecting tech selloff, tariff concerns, and mega-cap tech rotation
Recent 6-month regime shows near-zero spread drift (+0.004%/day), with tighter volatility (std ~1.1pp vs 1.76pp full history), suggesting lower-dispersion regime
FOMC meeting March 17-18 is a major catalyst at period start. Markets divided on rate cut vs hold. Adds event volatility but ambiguous direction for NQ-ES spread
NQ beta to ES is ~1.3 in recent months, amplifying broad market moves. In uncertain macro environment with tariff risks, higher beta introduces slight negative skew to spread in risk-off scenarios
Q1 2026 prior biweekly spreads: +0.09, +0.08, -1.72, +0.25 pp. Three near zero, one large negative outlier (Feb 2-14 tech selloff). Median near zero with negative tail risk

reasoning:
**Forecast summary for NQ-ES spread (Mar 16–27, 2026):**

| Percentile | Value (pp) |
|-----------|-----------|
| 10th | -1.85 |
| 20th | -1.26 |
| 40th | -0.47 |
| 60th | +0.23 |
| 80th | +1.09 |
| 90th | +1.78 |

**Key drivers:**
- **Center slightly negative (~-0.1pp):** Recent 3-month regime shows NQ underperforming ES, driven by tech rotation and tariff headwinds
- **Wide uncertainty (80% CI: -1.85 to +1.78):** The FOMC meeting on March 17-18 sits right at the period start and markets are divided on a rate cut — this is a high-variance catalyst
- **NQ beta ~1.3:** Tech-heavy NQ amplifies broad market moves, creating asymmetric spread risk depending on overall market direction
- **Historical context:** Full 2020-2026 history shows long-run NQ outperformance (+0.21pp mean), but Q1 2026 has been mixed with one sharp -1.72pp episode during the Feb tech selloff

resolution_criteria:
This question is a subquestion of a group question. This subquestion specifically pertains to (and resolves according to) the option 'Mar 16 - Mar 27'. The resolution criteria for the parent question is below. 

Each subquestion will resolve as the difference between the percent total price return of [Nasdaq-100 Futures (NQ)](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/NQ%3DF/history/) and that of [S\&P 500 Futures (ES)](https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/ES%3DF/history/) for the corresponding biweekly period, according to Yahoo Finance data.

Specifically, each subquestion will resolve as NQ\_return - ES\_return.

Returns will be calculated as follows: If P₀ is the Close price of a company stock on the last trading day or half-day *before* the start of the period and P₁ is the Close price of the stock on the last trading day *of* the designated period, the percentage return will be calculated as:

$$
 \text{return} = 100 \times \frac{P1 - P0}{P0}
$$
background_info:
[Nasdaq-100](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nasdaq-100) focuses exclusively on 100 of the largest non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq exchange, heavily weighted towards technology and innovation. This index includes tech titans like Apple (approximately 12-15% of the index), Microsoft, Amazon, NVIDIA, and Alphabet (Google), making it a prime indicator of the tech sector's performance and innovation economy.

[S\&P 500](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/S%26P_500) represents 500 of the largest publicly traded U.S. companies across diverse sectors. It captures the broader market sentiment, including technology, healthcare, financial, and industrial giants like Apple, Microsoft, Amazon, JPMorgan Chase, and Johnson & Johnson. The index is market-cap weighted, meaning larger companies have a more significant impact on its movement.
